parentQuestion: Post-parotid surgery: cannot smile or close eye, but CAN feel face

scaffolds.q: The parotid gland is a salivary gland near your ear and jaw.
scaffolds.explain: The parotid produces saliva. The facial nerve runs through it.

scaffolds.q: Surgery near a nerve can accidentally injure that nerve.
scaffolds.explain: Nerves near surgical sites are at risk of damage during procedures.

scaffolds.q: Smiling and closing your eye are facial movements.
scaffolds.explain: These are controlled by the facial nerve (CN VII).

scaffolds.q: Feeling touch on your face is a different function than moving your face.
scaffolds.explain: Sensation (CN V) and movement (CN VII) use different nerves.

scaffolds.q: CN VII (facial) controls facial MOVEMENT, CN V (trigeminal) detects facial SENSATION.
scaffolds.explain: Different nerves do different jobs. That is why one can be damaged while the other works.

scaffolds.q: The facial nerve passes through the parotid gland.
scaffolds.explain: CN VII runs through the parotid, making it vulnerable during parotid surgery.

scaffolds.q: If only movement is lost but sensation is intact, only the motor nerve is damaged.
scaffolds.explain: Right! CN VII (motor) is damaged, CN V (sensory) is intact.

scaffolds.q: CN VII provides motor innervation to facial muscles but not sensation.
scaffolds.explain: Facial movement is CN VII. Face sensation is CN V.

scaffolds.q: Parotid surgery risks facial nerve injury because CN VII traverses the gland.
scaffolds.explain: The facial nerve branches run through the parotid gland.
